4.2Configuration
4.2.1Frontmatter4.2.2Layouts4.2.3Themes
4.3Directives

Directives

In Marque, anything that starts with @ is treated as a directive.

What is actually useful

  • @row: horizontal layout container
  • @column: vertical stack container (cards on top of each other)
  • @card + @callout: core content primitives
  • @container <name>: neutral scope/grouping block
  • @step ... @end step: block step item
  • Label: inline button/link directive
  • <span class="mq-badge class">Text</span>: inline badge directive
  • <span class="mq-divider-inline" aria-hidden="true" style="display:inline-block;vertical-align:middle;width:1.8rem;height:2px;margin:0 .35rem;background:var(--mq-primary,currentColor);opacity:.75;border-radius:2px;"></span>: block or inline divider

Two directive families

1) Block directives

Block directives define page structure and layout.
They use a start tag and an end tag.

Block directives

Examples: @container, @row, @card, @callout.

They're defined by a start tag (@<name>) and end tag (@end <name>), with content in between.

mq
@row features # begin block directive calling @<name>
  @card .accent
    ## Left card
    This is a card inside a row.
  @end card

  @card
    ## Right card
    This is another card inside the same row.
  @end card
@end row # end block directive with @end <name>

Inline directives

Inline directives don't require a separate end tag.

Examples: <a href="url" class="mq-btn primary">Label</a> button syntax, <span class="mq-badge ok">Text</span> badges, and <span class="mq-divider-inline" aria-hidden="true" style="display:inline-block;vertical-align:middle;width:1.8rem;height:2px;margin:0 .35rem;background:var(--mq-primary,currentColor);opacity:.75;border-radius:2px;"></span> inline.

mq
This is a <a href="/docs/directives.html" class="mq-btn primary">link</a> with button styling.
Here is a badge: <span class="mq-badge accent">New</span>.
This works inline too: before @divider after

This is a link with button styling.
Here is a badge: New.
This works inline too: before after

Container scope (recommended)

@container is a neutral block scope. It does not add layout or styles by itself.

Use it to group content semantically, then compose with @row, @card, or markdown inside.

Minimal example:

Welcome

Use this scope to group related blocks.

Fast setup.

Plain static output.

Welcome
Use this scope to group related blocks.

Fast setup.

Plain static output.

Notes

  • @stat exists but is optional. Use it only for KPI-like blocks.
  • <div class="mq-spotlight"></div> and @section are wrappers useful when your theme styles them.
  • @hero still works as a backward-compatible alias for <div class="mq-spotlight"></div>.
  • @tabs, @steps, and @line are removed.
  • @step is a block directive and is closed with @end step.

Custom directives (dev)

You can add your own directives by creating files in directives/ and registering them with defineDirective(...).

No parser core edit is needed, no renderer switch-case is needed.

Example registration:

js
module.exports = ({ defineDirective }) => {
  defineDirective('product-card', {
    type: 'block',
    render: ({ mods, name, children, ctx }) => {
      const variant = ctx.escapeAttr(mods[0] || 'default');
      const title = ctx.escapeAttr(name || '');
      return `<product-card variant="${variant}" title="${title}">${children}</product-card>`;
    },
  });

  defineDirective('sparkle', {
    type: 'inline',
    render: () => '<span class="mq-sparkle" aria-hidden="true">*</span>',
  });
};

Example usage in .mq:

mq
@product-card .featured Starter
## Starter Plan
Best for small teams.
@end product-card

Add some shine @sparkle

Starter Plan

Best for small teams.

Add some shine ✶

✶

Tip: directive names with hyphens are supported (product-card, my-alert, etc.).

Axis quick reference

  • @row lays children horizontally.
  • @column lays children vertically.

Example:

Top in left column

Bottom in left column

Top in right column

Step counter scope

Inside a @container, each @step ... @end step is numbered from 1 to n.
When you leave that container, numbering resets for the next container.

Example:

Install dependencies

Start dev server

Build for production

Upload dist/

For Developers

This allows you to have multiple independent step sequences on the same page, which is useful for things like "Setup steps" and "Publishing steps" that are conceptually separate.
